topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting irritation. I stared at the printed memo, the words about the Senior Manager role mocking me with their impartiality. Sarah had the experience, the tenure, and frankly, the polish. I’d put in those late nights, the extra projects, the sheer grit, yet here I was, watching her name penciled in before mine. My jaw tightened until a dull ache radiated through my temples. I slammed my lukewarm coffee mug onto the desk, the ceramic rattling loudly. "Unbelievable," I muttered, the sound rough in the quiet cubicle. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal against the linoleum sounding like a declaration of war. I needed to talk to Mark, to demand an explanation for this blatant disregard for merit.

[story 2]

My hands were shaking slightly as I reread the performance review summary. Every bullet point detailing my contributions felt like a deliberate misdirection. I clenched my fists so tightly my knuckles turned white, the pressure building behind my eyes until I could barely focus on the screen. When Sarah announced the decision in the conference room, her smile was too bright, too easy. I felt a sudden, hot rush in my chest, like trapped steam trying to escape. I didn't speak immediately. Instead, I took a slow, deliberate sip of water, letting the silence stretch, heavy and suffocating. When I finally spoke, my voice was dangerously level, "I see. Well, I certainly hope the criteria used for this selection were as robust as the results suggest." The air in the room seemed to thicken instantly.

[story 3]

I paced the length of my small office, my steps quick and uneven, the rhythm of my movement mirroring the frantic beat inside my chest. Every time I glanced toward Sarah’s desk, a sharp, unpleasant tension coiled in my gut. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a burning resentment that felt corrosive. I stopped abruptly by the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, my breath coming in short, sharp gasps. I wanted to shout, to tear the decision apart, but I kept my mouth shut, forcing myself to regulate the tremor in my hands. "This isn't fair," I whispered to the glass, the sound swallowed by the distant traffic. The feeling of being overlooked, of having all my efforts dismissed so casually, left me vibrating with barely contained energy.
